class_schedule.topic: How Computers Work: Hardware, Software, and the Internet
learning_objectives: Identify the major hardware components of a computer and their functions
Distinguish between system software and application software
Explain how the internet and web browsers deliver content
readings: Computer Concepts and Microsoft 365 Ch. 1-2 by Parsons
How the Internet Works in 5 Minutes (supplementary video transcript)

lecture_notes.introduction: Computers are tools, and understanding how tools work makes you a more effective user. This course demystifies the technology you use daily and builds the skills employers expect every graduate to have.
key_concepts: CPU, RAM, storage (HDD vs. SSD): what each does and why it matters
Operating systems: the software that manages everything else
Application software vs. system software
How the internet works: IP addresses, DNS, HTTP, and web servers
Cloud computing: what it means and why it matters
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live tour of a computer's internals using a transparent case or teardown images, followed by a traceroute showing how data travels across the internet

review.value: CPU is the processor that executes instructions; RAM is temporary fast memory for active tasks. More RAM lets you run more programs simultaneously.
You type a URL, DNS translates it to an IP address, your browser sends an HTTP request to that server, the server responds with HTML/CSS/JS, your browser renders the page.

class_schedule.topic: File Management and Operating System Essentials
learning_objectives: Organize files using folders, naming conventions, and file extensions
Perform essential operating system tasks: search, install, update, and manage settings
Understand file paths and the difference between local and cloud storage

lecture_notes.introduction: Good file management is the digital equivalent of keeping an organized desk. Students who cannot find their files waste hours every semester. This session builds habits that prevent that.
key_concepts: File systems: directories, subdirectories, and paths
File naming conventions: descriptive, dated, version-controlled
Common file extensions and their associated applications
Local storage vs. cloud storage: OneDrive, Google Drive, iCloud
Backup strategies: the 3-2-1 rule
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students organize a messy download folder using a naming convention and folder hierarchy system
setup_requirements: Sample messy files folder (provided via LMS download)

review.value: A path describes the location of a file: C:\Users\Name\Documents\course\essay.docx. Understanding paths is essential for troubleshooting 'file not found' errors.
Keep 3 copies of important files, on 2 different types of media, with 1 copy stored offsite (cloud counts as offsite).

learning_objectives: Create professionally formatted documents using styles, headings, and sections
Use templates, headers/footers, and page numbering
Apply track changes and comments for collaborative editing
readings: Computer Concepts and Microsoft 365 Ch. 5 (Word section)

lecture_notes.introduction: Word processing is the most used computer skill in academic and professional settings. This session goes beyond basic typing to teach the formatting and collaboration features that produce polished, professional documents.
key_concepts: Styles and headings: consistent formatting with one click
Page layout: margins, orientation, sections, and page breaks
Headers, footers, and automatic page numbering
Table of contents generation from heading styles
Track changes and comments for peer review and collaboration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Formatting a raw text document into a properly styled APA research paper in real time
setup_requirements: Raw unformatted text file for practice (provided)

review.value: Styles apply consistent formatting across a document and enable automatic table of contents. Manual formatting (bold, font size) must be changed one instance at a time.
Records every edit made to a document so collaborators can accept or reject changes. Essential for group writing and editorial review.

class_schedule.topic: Spreadsheets: Formulas, Functions, and Data Organization
learning_objectives: Create formulas using cell references and basic operators
Apply functions: SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, MIN, MAX, IF
Organize data using sorting, filtering, and conditional formatting
readings: Computer Concepts and Microsoft 365 Ch. 6 (Excel section)

lecture_notes.introduction: Spreadsheets are the most powerful everyday tool most people never learn properly. Whether you manage a budget, analyze survey data, or plan a project, spreadsheet skills save hours of work.
key_concepts: Cell references: relative (A1) vs. absolute ($A$1)
Formulas: arithmetic operators and order of operations
Functions: SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, MIN, MAX for summarizing data
IF function for conditional logic in cells
Sorting, filtering, and conditional formatting for data exploration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a personal budget tracker from scratch with formulas, conditional formatting for overspending, and summary charts

review.value: Relative references (A1) change when you copy formulas. Absolute references ($A$1) stay fixed. Use $ to lock a reference when it should not change.
=IF(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false). Example: =IF(B2>100, 'Over Budget', 'OK') displays 'Over Budget' when the value exceeds 100.

learning_objectives: Create effective presentation slides using design principles
Use animations and transitions purposefully, not decoratively
Deliver a presentation using presenter view and notes
readings: Computer Concepts and Microsoft 365 Ch. 7 (PowerPoint section)
Presentation Zen Ch. 1-3 by Garr Reynolds

lecture_notes.introduction: Presentation software is a visual aid, not a document. The most common mistake is putting everything on the slides. This session teaches you to design slides that enhance your message instead of replacing you as the speaker.
key_concepts: Slide design principles: contrast, alignment, repetition, proximity
One message per slide: the billboard test
Choosing appropriate chart types for different data
Purposeful animations vs. distracting effects
Presenter view: seeing notes and upcoming slides while presenting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Before-and-after makeover of a text-heavy corporate slide deck into a visual presentation

review.value: If your slide cannot be understood in 3 seconds like a billboard, it has too much content. Move details to your spoken words or handouts.
Bar charts for comparisons, line charts for trends over time, pie charts only for parts of a whole (and only with few categories).

learning_objectives: Identify common cybersecurity threats: phishing, malware, social engineering
Create and manage strong passwords using best practices
Understand privacy settings and data protection in digital environments
readings: Have I Been Pwned: Understanding Data Breaches (supplementary article)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every connected device is a potential target. Cybersecurity is not just an IT concern; it is a personal responsibility. This session equips you to protect yourself, your data, and your institution from the most common threats.
key_concepts: Phishing: how to recognize fraudulent emails, texts, and websites
Malware types: viruses, ransomware, spyware, trojans
Password best practices: length, uniqueness, and password managers
Two-factor authentication: why and how to enable it
Privacy settings on social media and mobile devices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Identifying phishing attempts in a set of 10 real-world emails, followed by setting up a password manager
setup_requirements: Sample phishing emails (screenshots, provided in LMS)

review.value: Urgency, generic greetings, misspelled domains, requests for passwords or payments, mismatched sender addresses and display names
Use a unique password for every account, at least 12 characters, with a mix of types. A password manager is the only practical way to achieve this.

questions.question: Explain the difference between RAM and storage (SSD/HDD). Why does a computer need both? What happens when RAM is full?
questions.answer: RAM (Random Access Memory) is fast, temporary memory that holds data for currently running programs. It is cleared when the computer shuts down. Storage (SSD or HDD) is permanent memory that retains files even when power is off, but it is slower to access. A computer needs both because RAM provides the speed needed for active tasks while storage provides the capacity for long-term file retention. When RAM is full, the operating system uses a portion of storage as virtual memory (swap space), which is much slower and causes the computer to lag.

rubric.key: Correct distinction between RAM and storage
Explains what happens when RAM is full

questions.question: Describe the 3-2-1 backup rule and give a specific example of how a college student could implement it for their coursework.
questions.answer: The 3-2-1 rule means keeping 3 copies of important files on 2 different types of media with 1 copy stored offsite. Example for a student: Copy 1 is on the laptop's internal SSD (media type 1). Copy 2 is on an external USB drive kept in a desk drawer (media type 2). Copy 3 is automatically synced to Google Drive or OneDrive (offsite cloud storage, counting as media type 2 or a separate type). This protects against laptop failure (USB and cloud survive), USB loss (laptop and cloud survive), and local disasters like a dorm fire (cloud survives).

rubric.key: Specific student example with three copies
Explains what risks each copy protects against

questions.question: What is the difference between a relative cell reference and an absolute cell reference in a spreadsheet? Write a formula using each to calculate sales tax at 8% for a list of prices in column B, where the tax rate is in cell E1.
questions.answer: A relative reference changes when the formula is copied to another cell. An absolute reference (using $) stays fixed regardless of where the formula is copied. For a list of prices in B2:B10 with tax rate in E1: Using relative reference for the price and absolute for the tax rate: =B2*$E$1. When copied down to row 3, it becomes =B3*$E$1. The B reference adjusts to each row, but $E$1 always points to the tax rate cell. Without the absolute reference, =B2*E1 copied to row 3 would become =B3*E2, pointing to an empty or wrong cell.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of relative and absolute references
Explains what goes wrong without absolute reference

questions.question: You receive an email from 'security@your-university-alert.com' saying your account will be locked in 24 hours unless you click a link to verify your password. List four red flags that identify this as a phishing attempt.
questions.answer: (1) The sender domain 'your-university-alert.com' does not match the real university domain; it is a lookalike. (2) Urgency and threats ('locked in 24 hours') pressure you into acting without thinking. (3) Legitimate institutions never ask you to verify your password by clicking an email link. (4) The email asks for sensitive credentials (password), which a real security team would never request via email. Additional red flags could include: generic greeting instead of your name, grammatical errors, or a link URL that does not match the university's actual website when hovered over.

rubric.key: Password request via email recognized as illegitimate

major_assignments.title: Digital Skills Capstone Project
major_assignments.description: Demonstrate mastery of all computer literacy skills by creating a complete project that integrates word processing, spreadsheet analysis, presentation design, and cybersecurity awareness. Choose a real-world scenario (small business plan, event planning, or personal financial plan) and produce professional deliverables using each tool.
objectives: Create a professionally formatted document using styles and proper layout
Build a functional spreadsheet with formulas, functions, and charts
Design a compelling presentation following visual communication principles
Apply cybersecurity best practices to protect project files and accounts
deliverables: Written report (1000-1500 words) in Word/Docs with proper formatting, styles, and table of contents
Spreadsheet with at least 10 formulas/functions, conditional formatting, and 2 charts
Presentation (8-10 slides) following slide design principles with speaker notes
Cybersecurity checklist documenting how you secured your accounts and files for this project
All files organized in a named folder structure following file management conventions

criteria: Heading styles applied consistently
Automatic table of contents generated
Headers, footers, and page numbers present
Professional layout and error-free content
At least 10 formulas/functions used correctly
Conditional formatting highlights key data
Two charts with appropriate types and labels
Data is organized and clearly labeled
One message per slide with visual support
Consistent design theme throughout
Speaker notes demonstrate depth beyond slides
Charts and images support the narrative
Files organized in logical folder structure with descriptive names
Cybersecurity checklist completed with specific actions taken
Password manager or strong unique passwords documented
Two-factor authentication enabled on relevant accounts
All four deliverables completed to professional standards
Skills from each topic area applied correctly and purposefully
Real-world scenario is realistic and demonstrates practical value
File organization and security practices demonstrate digital competence

hands_on_labs.description: Weekly lab exercises practicing specific software skills

quizzes.description: Assessment of computer concepts and vocabulary

capstone_project.description: Integrated project demonstrating all course skills in a real-world scenario

skill_demonstrations.description: Timed skill demonstrations showing proficiency in each software tool

cybersecurity_portfolio.description: Documentation of personal cybersecurity improvements made during the course

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are hands-on and cannot be replicated from notes alone. More than 3 absences will result in a 5% grade reduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Lab exercises are due by the end of the lab session. Late submissions receive 50% credit for one day, zero after that. The capstone project deadline is firm.
course_policies.collaboration: Students may help each other with software techniques during labs. All submitted work must be your own. Submitting another student's files is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Do not share completed lab files with other students. Each student's work should reflect their own learning process.
course_policies.technology_requirements: A personal computer or reliable access to campus computer labs. Microsoft 365 is provided free through the university. Google Workspace is an acceptable alternative for all assignments.
